questions.question: What is the primary motivation of a Steady (S) personality?

options.text: Power and Control
Status and Fun
Correctness and Logic
Stability and Harmony

questions.explanation: S-types value safety, predictability, and keeping everyone happy. They dislike conflict and sudden change.

questions.question: Why are S-types often called 'The Silent Killer' of startup deals?

options.text: They act aggressive and hang up on you
They are polite and listen, but refuse to buy because they fear change
They steal your intellectual property
They talk too much and waste time

questions.explanation: Startups sell change. S-types fear change. Because they are polite, they won't tell you 'No' to your face; they just stall indefinitely.

questions.question: Which closing technique works best with an S-type?

options.text: The Urgency Close ('Price goes up tomorrow!')
The Assumptive Close ('When do you want delivery?')
The Support Close ('Here is the step-by-step plan for how we help you migrate')
The Shame Close ('Your competitors are beating you')

questions.explanation: S-types need to feel safe. Showing a clear, supported path to implementation removes the fear of the unknown.

questions.question: If an S-type says, 'I need to check with the team,' what does this usually mean?

options.text: It is a lie to get off the phone
They are buying time to find a competitor
They genuinely value consensus and fear upsetting their colleagues
They are not the decision maker

questions.explanation: S-types operate on consensus. They do not want to force a decision that makes their team unhappy.

questions.question: When pitching an S-type, you should emphasize:

options.text: Disruption and Revolution
Evolution and Safety
Speed and Risk
Innovation and Breaking things

questions.explanation: 'Revolution' implies chaos. 'Evolution' implies a safe, managed improvement. S-types buy Evolution.
